news_summary: Innovative scientific methods and community efforts are advancing wildlife monitoring, conservation of endangered species, and understanding of Arctic sea ice.
news_summary_fr: Des méthodes scientifiques innovantes et les efforts de la communauté permettent de faire progresser la surveillance de la faune sauvage, la conservation des espèces menacées et la compréhension de la banquise arctique.
news_summary_es: Los métodos científicos innovadores y las iniciativas comunitarias están impulsando el seguimiento de la fauna silvestre, la conservación de las especies en peligro de extinción y la comprensión del hielo marino del Ártico.

articles.title: How environmental DNA turned river water into a global wildlife census
articles.summary:
There’s something almost absurd about how we’ve always measured wildlife. Two trained ecologists visit the same river, spend days cataloguing what they can see, and come back with completely different species lists. Neither is wrong. The data just can’t be compared, which means it can’t really be used. “If you and I went to the […]
The post How environmental DNA turned river water into a global wildlife census first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
There’s something almost absurd about how we’ve always measured wildlife. Two trained ecologists visit the same river, spend days cataloguing what they can see, and come back with completely different species lists. Neither is wrong. The data just can’t be compared, which means it can’t really be used.

“If you and I went to the same river, we would not produce the same species list,” says Dimple Patel, CEO of NatureMetrics. “This makes it very difficult to bring together data sets that people are actually able to reconcile as well as standardise on a global basis.”

Instead, her company began building with just a simple bottle of water.

Every living thing leaves a genetic trace

Everything that lives near water sheds DNA into it: skin cells, saliva, traces that linger for days to weeks. That’s environmental DNA, or eDNA. One liter of river water contains enough to identify every species that passed through recently.

“Every living organism will shed DNA into its environment,” Patel says. “From that litre of river water, we will then be able to map back each of those traces of DNA back to the fish, the amphibians, the mammals, the insects that they started from.”

NatureMetrics ships sampling kits anywhere in the world. No specialist training. Patel’s team wanted to test just how simple the process could be, so they handed a kit to a five-year-old. “She got excellent results.”

The filter goes back to a lab, where DNA sequencing technology, the same used in forensic science, identifies every species in the sample. No trapping, no netting. The ecosystem isn’t touched. “It takes a fraction of the time, a fraction of the cost, but gives you an incredibly accurate and rich data set,” Patel says.

The significance of this moment

Freshwater species populations have fallen 84 percent since 1970. More than half of global GDP depends on nature in some form. Degraded soil threatens food supply chains, and losing natural flood barriers puts communities at real risk. Measuring all of that, at scale, in a way that holds up across sites and researchers, wasn’t really possible before now.

NatureMetrics has processed samples in 116 countries, working with more than 600 organizations. This year it hit a milestone: 10 percent of Earth’s surface surveyed using eDNA. The platform maps species detections, tracks change over time, and can show whether restoration work in a degraded area is doing anything.

The clients include WWF and conservation groups, but also mining companies, energy producers, and agricultural supply chains. Consumer goods companies have been using the data to understand the bacteria and fungi that make food production possible at all.

“How can we on a biological level help nurture the soil that is going to continue to give us food for the next 50 years?” Patel asks.

Nature on the balance sheet

NatureMetrics was recognized as an Earthshot Prize finalist a couple years ago. The prize, founded by the Prince of Wales, has given Patel something useful when talking to industries that are slow to change: proof the science has been reviewed by someone other than the company selling it.

“Having someone like the Earthshot Prize, where you know they have done due diligence — being able to say we’re supported by them, they trust our technology — it really opens a lot of doors,” she says.

The doors she wants open are in corporate boardrooms. Patel wants biodiversity to move out of field science and into finance, to show up on the same documents where companies account for what they own and what they owe.

“We want nature to be on balance sheets,” she says. “We want organisations and companies to be actually valuing the impact they’re having on nature and accounting for that in the way that they operate their businesses and make their decisions.”

The data to make that possible already exists. The harder part is getting the people who run large companies to treat it as something worth looking at. “We’re looking to give nature a spot in the boardroom,” Patel says.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant environmental breakthrough using environmental DNA (eDNA) to accurately and efficiently monitor wildlife biodiversity globally. This method provides a scalable, standardized, and non-invasive way to track species and ecosystem health, with broad implications for conservation, corporate environmental accountability, and sustainable resource management. The story is detailed, focused, and highlights measurable positive impact on environmental monitoring and biodiversity protection worldwide.
articles.personality_title: How river water DNA is changing wildlife monitoring worldwide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, ecologists trying to count wildlife in rivers faced a big problem. Different experts visiting the same river would find different animals, making it hard to compare or trust the results. This made it difficult to track and protect species accurately.

**What happened** – A company called NatureMetrics found a new way to solve this problem using environmental DNA, or eDNA. Every living thing near water leaves tiny bits of DNA behind, like skin cells or saliva. By collecting just one liter of river water, scientists can identify all the animals that have been there recently. NatureMetrics sends simple water-sampling kits worldwide that anyone can use, even children. The samples are sent to a lab where advanced DNA testing reveals every species present, without disturbing the ecosystem.

**Impact** – This method is faster, cheaper, and more precise than traditional wildlife surveys. It has already been used in 116 countries, covering 10 percent of Earth’s surface. This helps scientists and organizations like WWF track changes in wildlife populations and check if environmental restoration is working. It also supports companies in industries like mining, energy, and farming to understand their effects on nature and manage resources better.

**What's next step** – NatureMetrics wants businesses to include nature’s health in their financial decisions, treating biodiversity like an important asset on their balance sheets. This could encourage companies to protect the environment more seriously. The challenge is to get more corporate leaders to use this data when making plans and policies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using DNA from river water, NatureMetrics is making wildlife monitoring easier and more accurate, helping protect nature worldwide and encouraging companies to value biodiversity in their decisions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment l’ADN de l’eau des rivières révolutionne le suivi de la faune mondiale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant longtemps, les écologistes ont eu du mal à compter les animaux dans les rivières. Deux experts visitant la même rivière ne trouvaient pas toujours les mêmes espèces, rendant les résultats difficiles à comparer et à utiliser.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une entreprise appelée NatureMetrics a trouvé une nouvelle solution grâce à l’ADN environnemental, ou eADN. Chaque être vivant près de l’eau laisse des traces d’ADN, comme des cellules de peau ou de la salive. En prélevant seulement un litre d’eau de rivière, les scientifiques peuvent identifier tous les animaux présents récemment. NatureMetrics envoie des kits de prélèvement simples à utiliser partout dans le monde, même par des enfants. Les échantillons sont analysés en laboratoire avec une technologie avancée qui révèle toutes les espèces sans perturber l’écosystème.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est plus rapide, moins coûteuse et plus précise que les enquêtes classiques. Elle a déjà été utilisée dans 116 pays, couvrant 10 % de la surface de la Terre. Cela aide les scientifiques et des organisations comme le WWF à suivre les populations d’animaux et à vérifier si les efforts de restauration fonctionnent. Cela aide aussi des entreprises dans les secteurs minier, énergétique et agricole à mieux comprendre leur impact sur la nature.

**Prochaine étape** – NatureMetrics souhaite que les entreprises intègrent la santé de la nature dans leurs décisions financières, en considérant la biodiversité comme un actif important dans leurs bilans. Cela pourrait encourager les entreprises à mieux protéger l’environnement. Le défi est de convaincre plus de dirigeants d’utiliser ces données dans leurs plans.

**Une phrase clé** – Grâce à l’ADN provenant de l’eau des rivières, NatureMetrics facilite et améliore le suivi de la faune, aidant à protéger la nature dans le monde et incitant les entreprises à valoriser la biodiversité dans leurs décisions.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo el ADN del agua de los ríos está cambiando el monitoreo de la vida silvestre en todo el mundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, los ecólogos tuvieron problemas para contar animales en los ríos. Dos expertos que visitaban el mismo río no encontraban siempre las mismas especies, por lo que los resultados eran difíciles de comparar y usar.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa llamada NatureMetrics encontró una nueva solución con ADN ambiental, o eADN. Todos los seres vivos cerca del agua dejan pequeñas huellas de ADN, como células de piel o saliva. Con solo un litro de agua de río, los científicos pueden identificar todos los animales que estuvieron ahí recientemente. NatureMetrics envía kits de muestreo fáciles de usar en todo el mundo, incluso por niños. Las muestras se analizan en un laboratorio con tecnología avanzada para revelar todas las especies sin dañar el ecosistema.

**Impacto** – Este método es más rápido, barato y preciso que las encuestas tradicionales. Ya se ha usado en 116 países, cubriendo el 10 % de la superficie de la Tierra. Esto ayuda a científicos y organizaciones como WWF a seguir los cambios en poblaciones animales y verificar si las restauraciones funcionan. También ayuda a empresas en minería, energía y agricultura a entender mejor su impacto en la naturaleza.

**Próximo paso** – NatureMetrics quiere que las empresas incluyan la salud de la naturaleza en sus decisiones financieras, tratando la biodiversidad como un activo importante en sus balances. Esto podría motivar a las empresas a proteger más el medio ambiente. El reto es lograr que más líderes usen estos datos en sus planes.

**Una frase clave** – Usando ADN del agua de ríos, NatureMetrics facilita y mejora el monitoreo de la vida silvestre, ayudando a proteger la naturaleza y animando a las empresas a valorar la biodiversidad en sus decisiones.
articles.image_prompt: A gently flowing river with a translucent water bottle suspended above it, inside the bottle delicate DNA helix patterns intertwine with subtle outlines of diverse aquatic and terrestrial wildlife—fish, amphibians, mammals, and insects—glowing softly, all rendered in warm earthy tones and soft brushstrokes that evoke harmony between technology and nature.

articles.title: How beachgoers are turning snaps into science
articles.summary: Scientists hope to learn more about the impact of climate change on coasts through people's photos.
articles.content:
Beachgoers are being invited to become citizen scientists to help track how our coastline is changing.

Armed with nothing more than a smartphone, visitors to Tyrella Beach in County Down can now take part in a global project by snapping a photo through a fixed frame and logged via a website.

Each picture becomes part of a growing record that scientists will use to monitor how the beach shifts over time, helping them understand the effects of coastal erosion, rising sea levels and climate change.

Open days will be held to show people how to get involved.

As a geomorphologist with Geological Survey Northern Ireland, Melanie Biausque studies how coastlines change over time.

She is leading the project in Northern Ireland and said people who regularly visit the beach have the power to transform scientists' understanding of how it is changing.

"We're trying to understand how the coastline changes now and how it will evolve in the future, what can we do now to protect it so it's not too impacted by the changes that are going to happen in the environment."
articles.reasoning: The article describes a citizen science initiative that empowers the public to contribute to scientific monitoring of coastal changes due to climate change, providing meaningful environmental impact and broad public engagement with clear context and purpose.
articles.personality_title: Beach visitors help scientists track coastal changes with photos
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Coastal areas are changing because of things like rising sea levels and erosion. Scientists want to understand these changes better to protect beaches in the future.

**What happened** – At Tyrella Beach in Northern Ireland, visitors can take part in a project by taking photos through a special frame using their smartphones. These photos are then uploaded to a website, creating a record of how the coastline looks over time.

**Impact** – This project lets regular people help scientists by sharing pictures that show how the beach is changing. It gives researchers more information to study climate effects on coasts and helps them plan ways to protect the shoreline.

**What's next step** – Open days will be held to teach more people how to join the project. The collected photos will continue to build a detailed record that scientists can use to track changes and plan for the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – People visiting Tyrella Beach can use their phones to help scientists understand and protect changing coastlines caused by climate change.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les visiteurs de la plage aident les scientifiques à suivre les changements côtiers grâce à des photos
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les zones côtières changent à cause de la montée du niveau de la mer et de l’érosion. Les scientifiques veulent mieux comprendre ces changements pour protéger les plages à l’avenir.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – À la plage de Tyrella en Irlande du Nord, les visiteurs peuvent participer à un projet en prenant des photos à travers un cadre spécial avec leur smartphone. Ces photos sont ensuite téléchargées sur un site web, créant un enregistrement de l’aspect du littoral au fil du temps.

**Impact** – Ce projet permet aux gens ordinaires d’aider les scientifiques en partageant des images montrant l’évolution de la plage. Cela donne aux chercheurs plus d’informations pour étudier les effets du climat sur les côtes et les aide à planifier des moyens de protéger le littoral.

**Prochaine étape** – Des journées portes ouvertes seront organisées pour apprendre à plus de personnes comment participer au projet. Les photos collectées continueront à construire un dossier détaillé que les scientifiques utiliseront pour suivre les changements et préparer l’avenir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les visiteurs de la plage de Tyrella peuvent utiliser leur téléphone pour aider les scientifiques à comprendre et protéger les côtes qui changent à cause du changement climatique.
articles.personality_title_es: Los visitantes de la playa ayudan a los científicos a seguir los cambios en la costa con fotos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las zonas costeras están cambiando debido a la subida del nivel del mar y la erosión. Los científicos quieren entender mejor estos cambios para proteger las playas en el futuro.

**Qué pasó** – En la playa Tyrella en Irlanda del Norte, los visitantes pueden participar en un proyecto tomando fotos a través de un marco especial con sus teléfonos inteligentes. Estas fotos se suben a un sitio web, creando un registro de cómo se ve la costa con el tiempo.

**Impacto** – Este proyecto permite que la gente común ayude a los científicos compartiendo imágenes que muestran cómo cambia la playa. Esto da a los investigadores más información para estudiar los efectos del clima en las costas y les ayuda a planear cómo proteger la orilla.

**Próximo paso** – Se organizarán días abiertos para enseñar a más personas cómo unirse al proyecto. Las fotos recopiladas seguirán formando un registro detallado que los científicos usarán para seguir los cambios y planear para el futuro.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los visitantes de la playa Tyrella pueden usar sus teléfonos para ayudar a los científicos a entender y proteger las costas que cambian por el cambio climático.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene beach scene at Tyrella Beach with a simple wooden frame standing on the sand, framing a shifting shoreline where gentle waves meet eroding cliffs, alongside a smartphone silhouette capturing the scene, symbolizing community-driven coastal monitoring and the evolving landscape shaped by nature and climate.

articles.title: How a 40-year project helped save little terns
articles.summary: The marine bird is threatened by high tides and beachgoers, but volunteers give a helping hand.
articles.content:
Hidden in the scrapes, this little tern chick is camouflaged - and vulnerable - on a Norfolk beach.

But it is surrounded by a record 700 other ground nests due to a project set up to protect the threatened species 40 years ago.

With the help of local volunteers and wardens, who monitor breeding sites 24 hours a day, the future of the little tern is looking much brighter than it did back in 1986.

"I think without all the concerted effort of many, many people there's no way that the numbers would be increasing in the way they are now," said Finn Duncan, the community and volunteering officer on the RSPB Tern Around project in Norfolk and north Suffolk.

"Year by year now, we're getting more little tern fledglings off and away back to Africa - so that's really amazing."

Little terns travel 3,000 miles (4,828km) from west Africa to breed on the UK coast, but once here they face high tides, natural predators and the risk of being trampled by beachgoers and their dogs.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a successful 40-year conservation project that has significantly improved the survival prospects of the threatened little tern species through community volunteer efforts and protective measures. This has a meaningful positive impact on biodiversity and environmental conservation, benefiting the broader ecosystem and public awareness.
articles.personality_title: Forty years of teamwork boost little tern numbers on UK beaches
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The little tern is a small marine bird that travels thousands of miles from West Africa to the UK to breed. These birds nest on beaches, but their eggs and chicks are vulnerable to dangers like high tides, predators, and people walking nearby.

**What happened** – Since 1986, a 40-year project has worked to protect little terns on Norfolk and north Suffolk beaches. Volunteers and wardens watch over the nesting sites around the clock to keep the birds safe. Their careful efforts have helped the number of little tern nests grow to a record 700 this year.

**Impact** – Thanks to the project, more little tern chicks are surviving and growing strong enough to fly back to Africa. This is important because little terns were once threatened and their numbers were going down. The success shows how people working together can help protect a species and keep nature healthy.

**What's next step** – The team plans to continue their monitoring and protection efforts to maintain and hopefully increase the little tern population. The project also raises awareness about how human activities affect wildlife, encouraging beachgoers to be more careful near nesting areas.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Long-term volunteer efforts have significantly increased the survival of little terns on UK beaches, showing the power of community action in protecting wildlife.
articles.personality_title_fr: Quarante ans de travail d'équipe pour sauver les sternes naines sur les plages britanniques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La sterne naine est un petit oiseau marin qui voyage sur des milliers de kilomètres depuis l'Afrique de l'Ouest jusqu'au Royaume-Uni pour se reproduire. Ces oiseaux nichent sur les plages, mais leurs œufs et poussins sont vulnérables aux marées hautes, aux prédateurs et aux promeneurs.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Depuis 1986, un projet de 40 ans protège les sternes naines sur les plages de Norfolk et du nord de Suffolk. Des bénévoles et des gardiens surveillent les sites de nidification 24 heures sur 24 pour assurer la sécurité des oiseaux. Le nombre de nids a atteint un record de 700 cette année grâce à leurs efforts.

**Impact** – Grâce au projet, de plus en plus de poussins de sternes naines survivent et deviennent assez forts pour retourner en Afrique. Cela est important car ces oiseaux étaient autrefois menacés et leur nombre diminuait. Ce succès montre que la collaboration peut aider à protéger une espèce et préserver la nature.

**Prochaines étapes** – L'équipe prévoit de continuer la surveillance et la protection afin de maintenir et d'augmenter la population de sternes naines. Le projet sensibilise aussi le public à l'impact des activités humaines sur la faune, encourageant les visiteurs à respecter les zones de nidification.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les efforts bénévoles à long terme ont nettement augmenté la survie des sternes naines sur les plages britanniques, démontrant le pouvoir de l'action collective pour protéger la faune.
articles.personality_title_es: Cuarenta años de trabajo conjunto aumentan las poblaciones de charrancitos en las playas del Reino Unido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El charrancito es un pequeño ave marina que viaja miles de kilómetros desde África Occidental hasta el Reino Unido para reproducirse. Estas aves anidan en las playas, pero sus huevos y polluelos son vulnerables a las mareas altas, depredadores y a las personas que caminan cerca.

**Qué pasó** – Desde 1986, un proyecto de 40 años ha protegido a los charrancitos en las playas de Norfolk y el norte de Suffolk. Voluntarios y guardias vigilan los sitios de anidación las 24 horas para mantener a las aves seguras. Gracias a su trabajo, este año se alcanzó un récord de 700 nidos.

**Impacto** – Gracias al proyecto, más polluelos de charrancito sobreviven y crecen lo suficiente para volar de regreso a África. Esto es importante porque antes estas aves estaban amenazadas y sus números bajaban. El éxito muestra cómo el trabajo en equipo puede ayudar a proteger una especie y conservar la naturaleza.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea seguir con la vigilancia y protección para mantener y aumentar la población de charrancitos. El proyecto también ayuda a que la gente entienda cómo sus acciones afectan a la vida silvestre, animando a los visitantes a respetar las zonas de anidación.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los esfuerzos voluntarios a largo plazo han aumentado significativamente la supervivencia de los charrancitos en las playas del Reino Unido, demostrando el poder de la acción comunitaria para proteger la vida silvestre.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sandy Norfolk beach scattered with hundreds of small, camouflaged little tern nests, with delicate tern chicks sheltered among scrapes in the sand; around the nests, gentle silhouettes of watchful volunteers and wardens symbolized by softly glowing lanterns and protective nets, under a calm sky suggesting hope and renewal.

articles.title: NASA Scientists Take to Air and Space to Study Arctic Sea Ice
articles.summary: This month, engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California are testing a spacecraft sensor that will help measure how quickly Arctic sea ice is disappearing. And while that instrument won’t launch for another year, scientists started preparing for its use during a recent field campaign in the Canadian wilderness. Researchers spent two weeks […]
articles.content:
These four views were captured from a World War II-era aircraft in April 2026, when scientists used instruments aboard the plane to study Arctic sea ice. Their flights were timed to coincide with satellites passing overhead so the airborne and orbital data could be combined. NASA/JPL-Caltech

This month, engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California are testing a spacecraft sensor that will help measure how quickly Arctic sea ice is disappearing. And while that instrument won’t launch for another year, scientists started preparing for its use during a recent field campaign in the Canadian wilderness.

Researchers spent two weeks in April flying above the Arctic Ocean, often watching sunrise from an altitude of 1,500 feet (457 meters) in a World War II-era plane. A variety of cutting-edge sensors used to measure the thickness of sea ice and snow were aboard the plane, including a stand-in for the microwave radiometer now undergoing testing at JPL. Measuring sea ice thickness is tricky, requiring a number of precise figures, including how high the sea ice rises above water, the depth of snow on top of that ice, and microwave emissions from the surface.

Flights were timed to the passage of satellites overhead so coordinated observations could be taken of the same features. Combining the airborne and satellite data will improve scientists’ ability to measure sea ice and understand how climate conditions are evolving across the Arctic.

In recent decades, the extent and thickness of Arctic sea ice have changed. Improving measurements of those changes helps scientists better understand the Arctic system while supporting navigation, weather and ocean research, and future satellite observations. As Arctic shipping activity increases, the region is also becoming strategically and economically more significant.

According to Sahra Kacimi of JPL, who served as the field campaign’s science lead, ongoing warming in the Arctic could potentially impact public safety and economic interests.

Find out what Arctic sea ice looked like as scientists studied it from the air — and using space-based instruments — during a field campaign this past April.

Credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech

Frequent flyers

Kacimi has spent years studying sea ice using satellite data, but the top-down view she gets from space is different than peering out a plane’s window.

The bewildering diversity of sea ice creates otherworldly landscapes. The ice can be attached to land or adrift in the ocean; it can be rough or smooth. Driven by winds and ocean currents, the ice is constantly shifting, breaking apart, and deforming. Cracks can open into long stretches of exposed ocean, and collisions between floes can push ice rubble into massive ridges that extend for miles.

Some sea ice lasts only one season, while thicker ice can survive for several years (though multiyear sea ice is becoming less common in many parts of the Arctic). Entire ecosystems are affected by these changes, down to the arctic foxes and hares the scientists spotted throughout the trip.

Improving estimates of sea ice thickness helps scientists better understand how the region is changing and supports long-term observations of the Arctic environment. The NASA team logged about 50 hours in the air over the two-week campaign, conducting flights over drifting ice near the town of Inuvik before studying ice fixed to the shore of another location, a hamlet called Cambridge Bay.

For the Inuvik portion of the campaign, the team coordinated with the Surface Water and Ocean Topography (SWOT) mission, a satellite jointly developed by NASA and the French space agency, CNES (Centre National d’Études Spatiales), with JPL leading the United States component of the mission. Though it was designed to map the height of the globe’s sea and fresh water, SWOT can also measure the amount of sea ice above the waterline.

In Cambridge Bay, the NASA team joined researchers from ESA (European Space Agency), Germany’s Alfred Wegener Institute, and Canada’s University of Calgary. During this part of the campaign, coordinated flights soared over a field camp and under the tracks of satellite missions such as NASA’s Ice, Cloud, and Land Elevation Satellite-2 (ICESat-2) and ESA’s CryoSat-2.

To improve sea ice thickness estimates, ESA is developing, with cooperation from NASA, a new polar mission called Copernicus Polar Ice and Snow Topography Altimeter (CRISTAL). During the April airborne campaign, scientists flew instruments similar to what CRISTAL will carry, including the microwave radiometer now being tested at JPL.

“Combining observations from space, air, and ground surface instruments is essential for developing and validating algorithms for current and future missions,” Kacimi said.

For the scientists, it was also a chance to meet locals who see the Arctic’s changes up close. Kacimi spoke to community leaders and students at a STEM camp about how disappearing ice is affecting their communities.

“I’m used to looking at sea ice from space and thinking about its role in the global climate, but for people living in the Arctic, it carries a much deeper meaning,” Kacimi said.

Media Contacts

Andrew Good

Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif.

818-393-2433

andrew.c.good@jpl.nasa.gov

Liz Vlock

NASA Headquarters, Washington

202-358-1600

elizabeth.a.vlock@nasa.gov

2026-043
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's significant scientific efforts to improve measurement of Arctic sea ice thickness using airborne and satellite sensors. This work has broad environmental significance as it enhances understanding of climate change impacts in the Arctic, supports navigation and weather research, and involves international collaboration. The detailed description of the field campaign and its implications for climate science and local communities provides substantial context and demonstrates a positive real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA tests new sensor to track Arctic sea ice from air and space
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Arctic sea ice is melting faster due to climate change. Measuring how thick the ice is helps scientists understand these changes and their effects on nature and people.

**What happened** – In April 2026, NASA scientists flew a World War II-era plane over the Arctic Ocean near Canada. They used special instruments to study sea ice thickness and snow depth. These flights were planned to match satellite passes, so they could combine data from both air and space. Meanwhile, NASA engineers are testing a new microwave sensor that will launch on a spacecraft next year to improve these measurements.

**Impact** – This study helps make sea ice measurements more accurate. Knowing ice thickness better supports navigation, weather forecasts, and climate research. It also helps local Arctic communities who see changes in their environment every day. The project involved international partners from Europe and Canada, showing teamwork to study this important region.

**What's next step** – NASA will launch the new sensor on a satellite next year. Scientists will use it with other tools to keep tracking Arctic sea ice changes over time. They will continue working with communities to share information about the Arctic’s future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s new airborne and space tools are improving how we measure Arctic sea ice, helping us understand and respond to a changing Arctic environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA teste un nouveau capteur pour surveiller la glace de mer arctique depuis l’air et l’espace
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La glace de mer arctique fond plus rapidement à cause du changement climatique. Mesurer son épaisseur aide les scientifiques à comprendre ces changements et leurs effets sur la nature et les populations.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En avril 2026, des scientifiques de la NASA ont survolé l’océan Arctique près du Canada avec un avion de la Seconde Guerre mondiale. Ils ont utilisé des instruments spéciaux pour étudier l’épaisseur de la glace et la profondeur de la neige. Ces vols ont été coordonnés avec le passage de satellites pour combiner les données aériennes et spatiales. En parallèle, des ingénieurs testent un nouveau capteur micro-ondes qui sera lancé sur un satellite l’année prochaine pour améliorer ces mesures.

**Impact** – Cette étude permet d’obtenir des mesures de la glace plus précises. Mieux connaître l’épaisseur de la glace aide la navigation, les prévisions météo et la recherche sur le climat. Cela profite aussi aux communautés locales qui observent ces changements au quotidien. Ce projet rassemble des partenaires internationaux d’Europe et du Canada, montrant une collaboration mondiale pour étudier cette région importante.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA lancera le nouveau capteur sur un satellite l’année prochaine. Les scientifiques l’utiliseront avec d’autres outils pour suivre les changements de la glace arctique dans le temps. Ils continueront à travailler avec les communautés pour partager des informations sur l’avenir de l’Arctique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les nouveaux outils aériens et spatiaux de la NASA améliorent la mesure de la glace de mer arctique, aidant à mieux comprendre et réagir aux changements dans cette région.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA prueba un nuevo sensor para medir el hielo marino ártico desde el aire y el espacio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El hielo marino del Ártico se está derritiendo más rápido debido al cambio climático. Medir qué tan grueso es el hielo ayuda a los científicos a entender estos cambios y sus efectos en la naturaleza y las personas.

**Qué pasó** – En abril de 2026, científicos de la NASA volaron sobre el Océano Ártico cerca de Canadá en un avión de la Segunda Guerra Mundial. Usaron instrumentos especiales para estudiar el grosor del hielo y la profundidad de la nieve. Estos vuelos se coordinaron con el paso de satélites para combinar datos aéreos y espaciales. Al mismo tiempo, ingenieros de la NASA están probando un nuevo sensor de microondas que se lanzará en un satélite el próximo año para mejorar estas mediciones.

**Impacto** – Este estudio ayuda a obtener mediciones más precisas del hielo. Saber mejor el grosor del hielo apoya la navegación, el pronóstico del clima y la investigación climática. También beneficia a las comunidades locales que ven estos cambios cada día. El proyecto incluye colaboración internacional con socios de Europa y Canadá, mostrando trabajo conjunto para estudiar esta región vital.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA lanzará el nuevo sensor en un satélite el próximo año. Los científicos lo usarán junto con otras herramientas para seguir monitoreando los cambios en el hielo ártico. Continuarán trabajando con las comunidades para compartir información sobre el futuro del Ártico.

**Frase clave** – Las nuevas herramientas aéreas y espaciales de la NASA mejoran la medición del hielo marino ártico, ayudando a entender y responder a un Ártico cambiante.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vintage World War II-era airplane flying low over a vast, shifting Arctic sea ice landscape with textured ice floes, cracks revealing deep blue ocean water, and scattered snow patches, while abstract satellite orbits and sensor waves subtly weave above the scene, symbolizing the coordinated airborne and space-based scientific study of the melting Arctic ice.
